subject: History
title: The Making of a Global World

questions.text: The famous Silk Routes mainly connected:
questions.explanation: Silk routes were a vibrant network of trade and cultural links that bound together vast regions of Asia, Europe, and Northern Africa.

questions.text: Which disease proved most deadly in the conquest of America by Europeans?
questions.explanation: Smallpox, carried by European conquerors, decimated native American populations who had no immunity to it.

questions.text: The 'Rinderpest' was a devastating disease affecting:
questions.explanation: Rinderpest (cattle plague) arrived in Africa in the late 1880s, killing 90% of the cattle and destroying local livelihoods.

questions.text: The Bretton Woods Conference established which two institutions?
questions.explanation: The 1944 conference established the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank to preserve global economic stability.

questions.text: Most Indian indentured workers came from which regions?
questions.explanation: Indentured labor migration from India was largely from the present-day regions of eastern UP, Bihar, central India, and Tamil Nadu.

questions.text: Which food item is believed to have traveled from China to Europe to become spaghetti?
questions.explanation: Many of our common foods like potatoes, soya, and groundnuts were not known until after Columbus.

questions.text: The 'Irish Potato Famine' of the mid-1840s resulted in the death of how many people?
questions.explanation: Poor peasants in Ireland were so dependent on potatoes that they starved when disease destroyed the crop.

questions.text: Until the 18th century, which two countries were among the world's richest?
questions.explanation: They were also pre-eminent in Asian trade before the shift of power to Europe.

questions.text: The 'Corn Laws' in Britain were passed to:
options: Give free corn to the poor
questions.explanation: Pressure from landed groups forced the government to restrict imports to keep domestic prices high.

questions.text: The abolition of Corn Laws in Britain led to:
questions.explanation: Food could now be imported into Britain more cheaply than it could be produced within the country.

questions.text: What played a major role in transporting perishable foods over long distances in the late 19th century?
questions.explanation: This allowed meat from America and Australia to be sold in European markets.

questions.text: At the Berlin Conference of 1885, European powers met to:
questions.explanation: Most of the modern borders of Africa were drawn during this period of colonial expansion.

questions.text: In the 19th century, thousands of Indians and Chinese went to work on plantations and mines as:
questions.explanation: This was often described as a 'new system of slavery' under colonial rule.

questions.text: The 'Chutney music' popular in Trinidad and Guyana is a result of:
questions.explanation: It represents the creative expression of the cultural fusion among the migrant Indian population.

questions.text: The First World War was fought between which two power blocs?
questions.explanation: The Allies included Britain, France, and Russia; the Central Powers were Germany, Austria-Hungary, and Ottoman Turkey.

questions.text: The First World War is often called the first 'modern industrial war' because of the use of:
questions.explanation: The war saw the large-scale use of industrial technology for mass destruction.

questions.text: Which country transformed from being an international debtor to an international creditor after WWI?
questions.explanation: By the end of the war, the US and its citizens owned more overseas assets than foreign governments owned in the US.

questions.text: Who pioneered the 'assembly line' method of mass production in the car industry?
questions.explanation: He adapted the assembly line of a Chicago slaughterhouse to his new car plant in Detroit.

questions.text: The 'Great Depression' began around:
questions.explanation: It was a period of severe global economic decline that lasted until the mid-1930s.

questions.text: The IMF and World Bank are often referred to as the:
questions.explanation: They began operations in 1947 to manage the post-war global economy.

questions.text: Most developing countries organized themselves into the G-77 to demand a:
questions.explanation: They wanted a system that would give them real control over their natural resources and fairer trade.

questions.text: The system of 'fixed exchange rates' collapsed in the early 1970s and was replaced by:
questions.explanation: Currencies now fluctuate based on market supply and demand.

questions.text: El Dorado was a fabled city of:
questions.explanation: European explorers spent decades searching for this mythical city in South America.

questions.text: Canal Colonies in Punjab were settled to grow:
questions.explanation: Peasants from other parts of Punjab were settled on land irrigated by new canals.

questions.text: The famous Hosay carnival in Trinidad was a result of:
questions.explanation: It was a processed Muharram carnival in which workers of all races and religions joined.

questions.text: Which city became the global hub for finance and trade by the late 19th century?
questions.explanation: Britain was the world's leading industrial and financial power during this era.

questions.text: Indentured labour was officially abolished in India in:
questions.explanation: Opposition from Indian nationalist leaders eventually led to its abolition.

questions.text: Mass production became a feature of industrial production in which country first?
questions.explanation: The US led the shift towards assembly-line manufacturing in the early 20th century.

questions.text: Trade surpluses with India helped Britain pay its trade deficits with:
questions.explanation: Britain used the 'home charges' and trade surplus from India to balance its global accounts.

questions.text: The relocation of industry to low-wage countries like China was a result of:
options: The collapse of the Bretton Woods system
questions.explanation: This shift changed the world's economic geography starting in the late 1970s.

questions.text: Cowries were used as a form of ________ in the pre-modern world.
questions.explanation: These shells from the Maldives were used as money in many parts of the world.

questions.text: The 'Silk Route' was named so because of the dominance of ______ trade along it.
questions.explanation: Chinese silk cargoes were the most famous goods transported over these routes.

questions.text: Globalisation is primarily about:
questions.explanation: It involves the movement of goods, services, capital, and people across borders.

questions.text: The 'Global World' we live in today is a product of:
options: A long history of migration, trade, and conquest
questions.explanation: Modern global connectivity has deep historical roots spanning centuries.

questions.text: Potato was introduced to Europe from:
questions.explanation: It was one of the many crops brought to the Old World after the discovery of the Americas.

questions.text: Which crop's failure led to the Great Famine in Ireland?
questions.explanation: Irish peasants were so dependent on potatoes that its failure led to mass starvation.

questions.text: The discovery of ______ changed the global balance of power in the 16th century.
questions.explanation: Columbus's discovery opened up new routes and resources for European powers.

questions.text: Which of these was NOT a 'pre-modern' form of global contact?
questions.explanation: Air travel is a modern phenomenon, unlike the others which have existed for millennia.

questions.text: The flow of capital refers to the movement of:
questions.explanation: Economists identify three types of global 'flows': trade, labour, and capital.

questions.text: Rinderpest was also known as:
questions.explanation: It was a fast-spreading disease of cattle that had a terrifying impact on African livelihoods.
